journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: Perioperative dobutamine infusion in an African lion (Panthera leo)

contrib-group.aff: 1)Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, Seoul National University, Seoul, Republic of Korea
3)Seoul Wildlife Center, College of Veterinary Medicine, Seoul National University, Seoul, Republic of Korea

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Son W-G: carpeego@snu.ac.kr, Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2024 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p:
This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0:
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: Laparoscopic ovariectomy under general anesthesia was planned in a 10-year-old, 146 kg, apparently healthy female African lion (Panthera leo). The lion was immobilized via intramuscular darts containing midazolam (0.033 mg/kg), medetomidine (50 µg/kg) and ketamine (2.5 mg/kg), and intubated using an endotracheal tube (16 mm internal diameter). The anesthesia was maintained using sevoflurane (0.9–2.1% end-tidal concentration), in combination with remifentanil (0.1 µg/kg/min) and ketamine (11 µg/kg/min) at a constant rate infusion (CRI), with Hartmann’s solution (5 mL/kg/hr). Surgery was conducted with stable vital signs, but hypotension (mean arterial blood pressure 55 mmHg) developed, requiring dobutamine treatment. The hypotension was effectively controlled by adjusting dobutamine from 5 µg/kg/min to 0.2 to 0.3 µg/kg/min. This case suggests possibilities that dosages in this range can be clinically useful for peri-anesthetic hypotension in lions.

body.p: While most large felids are immobilized with dissociative anesthetics because of availability, cost, size, etc. [2, 7, 10, 14, 15, 17, 25], inhalation anesthesia depending on the health status of the animal and availability of anesthetic machine is recommended for procedures such as fracture repair, laparotomy, and any lengthy operations for the stability of the patient and the procedure. General anesthesia using inhalation anesthetics may necessitate hypotension management or symptomatic treatment, unlike dissociative anesthetics that generally stimulate sympathetic nerves [9, 18]. In such cases, dobutamine is a preferred drug for treating hypotension in various species. The appropriate clinical dose of dobutamine varies among species [16, 23, 26], and excessive use can lead to side effects such as seizures in cats, arrhythmia, tachycardia, hypertension, or hypotension [21]. Currently, there are limited reports on treatment of inhalation anesthesia induced hypotension in lions [1, 8], and an appropriate dosage of dobutamine has yet to be determined. This case report suggests the appropriate dose of dobutamine for a lion to control hypotension identified via the tongue artery during inhalational anesthesia for laparotomy.

Laparoscopic ovariectomy under general anesthesia was planned in a 10-year-old, 150 kg (assumed body weight), apparently healthy female African lion (Panthera leo) at the Seoul Zoo for population control. The lion’s anesthesia was performed without notable medical history, pre-anesthetic assessments (including physical examination and clinical diagnostic tests), and with limited information on age and weight. The animal was immobilized using a combination of an α2-adrenergic agonist, a dissociative drug, and a benzodiazepine. A mixture of medetomidine (50 µg/kg; JSK; Provet Veterinary Products Ltd., Istanbul, Turkey) and ketamine (2.5 mg/kg; Yuhan Corp., Seoul, Republic of Korea) was prepared in three anesthesia darts each with a total volume of 15 mL, and midazolam (0.033 mg/kg; Bukwang Pharmaceutical Corp., Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) was loaded into a dart at 4.95 mL [2, 19, 25]. Intramuscular injections were performed to the lion in the cage using a blowpipe and the darts. In succession, the first dart containing medetomidine-ketamine was injected into the shoulder, while the remaining three darts (containing midazolam in the second dart and medetomidine-ketamine in the others) were administered in the hip region. Two min after the last injection, the lion licked its lips and blinked slowly. To evaluate the depth of anesthesia, the ear pinna was gently stimulated with a stick and an ear flick reflex was observed [2]. Since the ear flick reflex disappeared after 10 min of the intramuscular injections, the trachea was intubated with an endotracheal tube with internal diameter 16 mm (Equine Silicone Endotracheal Tube 16 mm; Jorgensen Laboratories, Loveland, CO, USA). Blood pressure was monitored using an oscillometric blood pressure device (SunTech Vet25 NIBP®; SunTech Medical Inc., Morrisville, NC, USA) on the tail while transferring the lion from the cage to the hospital operating room. Oxygenation was supported by maintaining 4 L/min oxygen flow through oxygen tank and a bag-valve mask (Silicone Resuscitator MR010; MOW Medical, Wonju, Republic of Korea). The weight was measured at 146 kg after moving to the operating room, and the patient was positioned ventrodorsally on the operating table. The endotracheal tube was connected to the corrugated tube of the anesthesia machine (Carestation 620; GE Medical Systems Co., Ltd., Wuxi, China), and anesthesia was maintained for 163 min with 100% oxygen (flow rate 4 L/min) and sevoflurane (0.9–2.1% end-tidal concentration, measured with patient monitor, CARESCAPE Monitor B650; GE HealthCare, Chicago, IL, USA) via mechanical ventilator (Carestation 620; GE Medical Systems Co., Ltd., Wuxi, China). Intravenous catheterization was performed with a 16-gauge catheter (BD Angiocath Plus I.V. Catheter, 16GA; Becton Dickinson Korea Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) in the right anterior tibial vein, and administrations of Hartmann solution (5 mL/kg/hr; JW Pharmaceutical Corp., Gwacheon, Republic of Korea). A combination of remifentanil (0.1 µg/kg/min; Hana Pharm Co., Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) and ketamine (11 µg/kg/min) was administered [11] intraoperatively via constant rate infusion (CRI) using infusion pumps (IP-7700 Volumetric Infusion Pump; AMPall Co., Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea), which was planned as a balanced anesthetics to maintain a steady depth of anesthesia for a relatively long surgery. For patient monitoring, an oxygen saturation sensor (CARESCAPE Monitor B650; GE HealthCare, Chicago, IL, USA) was attached between the interdigit of the left forelimb, and the electrocardiogram pads (3M Monitoring Electrode 2225H; 3M Korea, Seoul, Republic of Korea) and line were attached after clipping the ventral parts of the thorax. The esophageal thermometer (CARESCAPE Monitor B650; GE HealthCare) was inserted through the esophagus. Following the installation of basic patient monitoring equipment (CARESCAPE Monitor B650; GE HealthCare, Chicago, IL, USA), arterial catheterization was performed for real-time blood pressure monitoring using an invasive blood pressure device (TruWave Pressure Transducers; Edwards Lifesciences, Santa Ana, CA, USA) and arterial blood sampling for blood gas analysis using a blood gas analyzer (ABL 90 FLEX; Radiometer Korea, Seoul, Republic of Korea). It was attempted in the dorsal pedal artery, which is the place where the arterial line is usually mounted, but the pulse was not found due to the thick skin of the individual, so it was catheterized with a 24-gauge catheter (BD Angiocath Plus I.V. Catheter, 24GA; Becton Dickinson Korea Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) in the lingual artery (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
The head of an intubated lion in the ventrodorsal position. An arterial catheter for invasive blood pressure measurement is placed in the lingual artery on the ventral side of the tongue (arrow).). At 17 min after starting sevoflurane inhalation, hypoventilation was confirmed (49.0 mmHg of arterial partial pressure of carbon dioxide; PaCO2), so the peak airway pressure was adjusted from 10 to 14 cmH2O, subsequent tests after 15 min confirmed that ventilation improved (37.6 mmHg of PaCO2). Monitoring revealed that most of the parameters were within normal ranges, but compared with the indicators of lions injected anesthetized, the body temperature and mean arterial blood pressure (MAP) were low [2, 17]. Due to using an esophageal thermometer designed for small animals, it was inferred that the body temperature might not have been accurately measured due to the limited size of the thermometer compared to the lion. When measured through the rectum, it was maintained at 36.8–38.8°C. Hypotension, MAP of 55 mmHg, was observed 30 min after incision (64 min after starting sevoflurane inhalation), therefore fluid rate was increased to 10 mL/kg/hr and a dobutamine (Hana Pharm Co., Ltd.) CRI of 5 µg/kg/min was initiated as a treatment. However, within 2 min of starting dobutamine CRI, MAP abruptly increased to over 220 mmHg, necessitating immediate cessation of dobutamine administration. For dobutamine 5 µg/kg/min, systolic arterial blood pressure (SAP) 100–273 mmHg, diastolic arterial blood pressure (DAP) 62–191 mmHg, MAP 72–224 mmHg, heart rate (HR) 52–89 bpm. Consequently, an abnormally high MAP of about 170 mmHg persisted for approximately 10 min. After that, it was confirmed that blood pressure decreased, and dobutamine CRI was administered again 52 min after incision. By adjusting the dobutamine CRI rate to a range of 0.2–0.3 µg/kg/min, the MAP was successfully maintained at 100 mmHg. For dobutamine 0.2–0.3 µg/kg/min, SAP 99–128 mmHg, DAP 66–90 mmHg, MAP 76–102 mmHg, HR 55–62 bpm (Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Cardiovascular parameters and rate of dobutamine administration according to anesthetic time (starts with 10 min after intramuscular drug administration). Dobutamine infusion was started to treat hypotension (<60 mmHg mean arterial blood pressure, MAP) at 5 µg/kg/min, and systolic arterial blood pressure (SAP) abruptly increased up to 273 mmHg. Then, the dobutamine rate was adjusted to 0.2–0.3 µg/kg/min. HR, heart rate; BPM, beats per minute; DAP, diastolic arterial blood pressure.). During dobutamine administration, the numerical range for each item was as follows: pH 7.15–7.22, PaCO2 37.6–47.1 mmHg, PaO2 474–550 mmHg, HCO3− 15.5–18.2 mmol/L. After the surgery, infiltration of 0.5% bupivacaine (Myungmoon Bupivacaine HCl 0.5% Inj.; Myungmoon Pharm. Co., Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) 1 mg/kg was applied to the suture site subcutaneously, and the arterial catheter was removed from the tongue. Medetomidine (20 µg/kg) and ketamine (1 mg/kg) were additionally administered intravenously before moving to the cage to prevent the lion from waking up after inhalation anesthesia was discontinued. After the patient’s spontaneous respiration was confirmed, the vaporizer was turned off and the endotracheal tube was extubated. The intravenous catheter was removed and the lion was moved to the cage. While moving the lion to the cage from the operating room, oscillometric blood pressure was monitored from the tail, and ventilation was supported with a bag-valve mask. After moving to the cage, atipamezole (0.25 mg/kg; Zoetis Korea Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea), flumazenil (10 µg/kg; Bukwang Pharmaceutical Corp., Ltd.), and naloxone (10 µg/kg; Samjin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Seoul, Republic of Korea) were administered intramuscularly. The tube connected to the oxygen tank was placed in front of the patient’s nose in the cage to provide oxygenation. Ten min after administration of antagonists, ear flick reflex was confirmed. The total anesthesia time was 236 min, and the patient recovered from anesthesia and surgery uneventfully and was handed over to the zookeeper.

Maintaining proper blood pressure during anesthesia is essential. However, determining the normal blood pressure range in large wild animals have significant challenges. Even when measuring blood pressure without general anesthesia in a specialized cage [27], psychological factors and sympathetic arousal cannot be excluded. Mostly, anesthesia in wild felids involves intravenous anesthesia using an anesthesia dart, and the blood pressure measured through non-invasive blood pressure tends to be measured higher. The drugs used in darts include dissociative anesthetics and alpha-2 adrenergic agonists [3, 5,6,7,8, 10, 12,13,14, 17], which have potent immobilization effects. They induce high blood pressure through cardiovascular arousal and peripheral vascular constriction causing increased afterload. Over the past 20 years, injectable anesthesia has been the predominant method for anesthetizing Panthera leo. Previous studies suggested MAP range in total intravenous anesthesia in Siberian tigers (Panthera tigris altaica) and ocelots (Leopardus pardalis), 119–156 and 96–113 mmHg, retrospectively [3, 22]. The limited use of inhalation anesthesia and the infrequent occurrence of hypotension, due to the absence of blood pressure monitoring and recording, may account for the lack of reported dobutamine use in Panthera leo. While many studies have suggested the definition of hypotension as MAP below 60 mmHg [20], there is not an accepted standardized value for hypotension and it is even more challenging in Panthera leo due to limited data. As in this case, inhalation anesthesia may be indicated when intubation is required for relatively lengthy procedures, lasting two hr or more, or when ventilation cannot be controlled by spontaneous breathing such as situations like increased abdominal pressure from laparoscopic surgical procedure. Under intravenous anesthesia, the MAP was maintained at a higher range (100–145 mmHg), whereas under inhalational anesthesia, it was lower (70–90 mmHg). In this case, the blood pressure was below the threshold. so dobutamine was used.

A female lion was anesthetized with several CRI drugs to provide anesthetic depth suitable for laparoscopic ovariectomy, and the occurred hypotension was treated at a dose of dobutamine 0.2–0.3 µg/kg/min. Determining the dosage of drugs that affect the cardiovascular system is challenging during anesthesia in wild animals that lack related data. Failure to set the appropriate dose of the drug, or using drugs commonly used in human medicine or small animal clinical practice without monitoring side effects, can cause cardiovascular instability. Dobutamine is a drug preferred to treat hypotension in humans (2–20 µg/kg/min), dogs (2–10 µg/kg/min), cats (1–5 µg/kg/min), horses (0.5–2 µg/kg/min), alpacas (4–8 µg/kg/min), ratites (2–5 µg/kg/min), and elephants (0.3–0.5 µg/kg/min), but there are no reports or research for clinical usage and appropriate dosage in lions [16, 23, 26]. In this case, the initial rate was used based on small animals, but it was confirmed in real-time through an arterial line mounted on the tongue that severe hypertension occurred acutely. As a result of readjusting the rate, it was confirmed that the appropriate rate for this individual was 0.2–0.3 µg/kg/min, which was close to the appropriate rate used in large animals such as elephants or horses, rather than in small animals.

Beta-1 receptor is distributed throughout the heart, and dobutamine is synthetic balanced catecholamine, which acts on beta-1 receptor, increasing cardiac contractility according to dose-dependent without significant change in peripheral vascular resistance. Thus, it is often used as a first choice because it has relatively fewer side effects than other sympathomimetics accompanied by other actions such as increasing systemic vascular resistance. Dopamine may have the same effect depending on speed, but in this case, dobutamine was used as the first choice because dobutamine has less effect on HR than dopamine and stably increases MAP [4]. Similar to adjusting the dose of a drug to account for body surface area, the starting dose of dobutamine may need to be reduced in animals with relatively large hearts. In addition, in large felids, many cases and studies of the use of inotropic are needed to treat low blood pressure in procedures that require sufficient anesthesia and analgesia, such as laparotomy or orthopedic surgery.

In general, catheterization can be performed in the dorsal pedal artery or caudal median artery of the tail to monitor continuous blood pressure and conduct multiple arterial blood gas analysis. There were also reports of performing catheterization on the dorsal pedal artery in Panthera leo [6, 24]. However, in this case, pulse was undetectable in the same part. Therefore, catheterization was performed in the lingual artery of the tongue where the pulse was detected, and continuous blood pressure monitoring during anesthesia and multiple arterial blood gas analysis was successfully performed. It seems that it is clinically possible to use alternative catheterization on the tongue, which is relatively free from various effects such as body condition scores or skin thicknesses that vary from individual. In this case, close patient monitoring with settings such as pain management, anesthesia depth control with CRI, and invasive blood pressure monitoring through tongue catheterization enabled immediate observation of the dose-dependent effects of dobutamine. It is important to note that this report is based on a single case, and as such, further research is warranted to validate and expand upon these findings.

The appropriate dosage of dobutamine CRI varies among species, but there is a lack of reported guidelines specifically for lions. Taking into account the overall hemodynamics in animals, this case suggests that a dosage of 0.2 to 0.3 µg/kg/min can effectively manage peri-anesthetic hypotension in lions. It also highlights the importance of avoiding excessive dosages, such as 5 µg/kg/min dose led to severe hypertension in this case. Given the limited number of reported cases in lions, additional research and investigation are essential to better understand the optimal dosing and responses to dobutamine in Panthera leo and to ensure safer and more informed anesthesia practices in the future.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

p.funding-source: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Promotion program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2021R1F1A104546013), the New Faculty Startup Fund from Seoul National University, and the BK21 FOUR program and Research Institute of Veterinary Science, College of Veterinary Medicine, Seoul National University. This research was also supported by the National Institute of Wildlife Disease Control and Prevention as “Specialized Graduate School Support Project for Wildlife Disease Specialists”.
